summary: The Future Horizons blocker prompt assumes Drizzle ORM, a `shared/schema.ts` schema location, and that `WebhookSubscription.secretKey` and `UserSession.sessionToken` are live, plaintext-at-rest secrets used by the application. Verification against the actual repository shows three material mismatches that require a human design decision before any encryption-at-rest work proceeds.

mismatches.name: ORM and schema location
mismatches.promptStated: TypeScript / Drizzle ORM with `shared/schema.ts` and `drizzle/**/*` migrations
evidence: ls shared / ls drizzle → both ENOENT
prisma/schema.prisma is the canonical schema file
mismatches.impact: The prompt's column-shape recommendation (`secret_key_ciphertext bytea`, `secret_key_iv bytea`, `secret_key_tag bytea`, etc.) maps to PostgreSQL via either ORM but the code-side helpers, migration syntax, and dual-read code path differ between Drizzle and Prisma. The full plan is implementable but the file paths and tooling differ from the prompt as written.

mismatches.name: WebhookSubscription.secretKey is dormant
mismatches.promptStated: Used to sign outbound webhook payloads (HMAC).
mismatches.actualState: Stored on the row, but never read to sign anything. Outbound webhook payloads are signed with the env-vared `WEBHOOK_SECRET`.
evidence: Search across server/src for `secretKey` returns 3 hits: server/src/api/webhooks/webhooks.schema.ts (Zod input validation only), server/src/repositories/webhookSubscription.repository.ts (Prisma CRUD), server/src/api/webhooks/webhooks.service.ts (CRUD handler).

server/src/utils/webhooks.ts:124 — `function signPayload(body) { return createHmac('sha256', WEBHOOK_SECRET).update(body).digest('hex'); }` — the signing key is the env var `WEBHOOK_SECRET`, NOT a per-subscription column.
No call site retrieves `secretKey` from a stored WebhookSubscription row.
mismatches.impact: Encrypting a column that nothing reads protects nothing. The right remediation requires a design choice between (a) wiring `secretKey` into the signer for true per-subscription HMAC keys (then encrypting at rest), or (b) dropping the column entirely so the API stops collecting secrets it does not use.

mismatches.name: UserSession is fully dormant
mismatches.actualState: Not referenced anywhere in `server/src`. Authentication is via Keycloak OIDC token validation in `server/src/middleware/auth.ts`; session state lives in the IdP, not in this database.
evidence: case-insensitive grep for `sessiontoken|session_token|usersession` across server/src returns ZERO hits.
server/src/middleware/auth.ts contains no read of UserSession or sessionToken — token validation is OIDC-based.
mismatches.impact: Encrypting a column that no auth path reads protects no live data. The right remediation requires a design choice between (a) deleting the dormant model in a follow-up migration once a 7-day bake confirms nothing reads it, or (b) accepting that the model is reserved for a future feature and explicitly leaving it inert.

stopConditions.matched: fh-blocker-prompt-v1 generic stop condition: `If you cannot produce evidence for any required gate, mark the prompt EXPLORATORY and persist findings via /api/evidence so the next run can graduate to production.`
stopConditions.failClosedConsideration: The prompt's hard guardrail #6 (fail closed: refuse to start if SECRET_ENCRYPTION_KEK is missing) is a strong constraint to bolt onto two columns that currently protect no live data. Adding the dependency without a corresponding live read path increases the deployment failure surface without a matching security gain. This is exactly the situation EXPLORATORY mode exists for — surface to a human first.

designOptions.comment: Each option corresponds to a concrete follow-on PR that the human reviewer can pick. Option labels reference docs/security/secrets-at-rest.md.

options.name: Encrypt as-is (literal prompt execution adapted to Prisma)
options.summary: Build envelope encryption per the prompt, migrate, dual-read, rotate. Keeps both dormant columns alive, encrypted.
options.verdict: Not recommended. Adds production complexity and a fail-closed env requirement to protect data nothing reads.

options.name: Wire secretKey into the HMAC signer first, then encrypt
options.summary: Make the per-subscription secret a real production feature: signPayload uses the subscription's key when one is set, falls back to WEBHOOK_SECRET otherwise. Then encrypt at rest in the same PR or the next.
options.verdict: Recommended for the WebhookSubscription side IF per-subscription HMAC is actually wanted. Otherwise prefer Option C.

options.name: Drop the dormant columns / model
options.summary: Stop accepting `secretKey` on the WebhookSubscription create path; drop the column. Drop the UserSession model entirely (or leave it in schema but unmapped). Two reversible Prisma migrations.
options.verdict: Recommended unless Option B's per-subscription HMAC is a live requirement. Removes the secret-at-rest risk by removing the secret-at-rest data.

options.name: Hybrid
options.summary: Drop UserSession (Option C for sessions). For webhooks, choose B if per-subscription HMAC is wanted, otherwise C.
options.verdict: Recommended overall. UserSession's removal is uncontroversial; the webhook decision is a real product choice.

remediationDeliverablesInThisPR: docs/security/secrets-at-rest.md (design-decision doc with the four options and acceptance criteria for each)
noCodeChangesInThisPR: Confirmed. No source file, schema file, migration, environment variable, or CI workflow is modified in this run. The PR is purely an EXPLORATORY artefact set.
